planting.note: Root softwood cuttings under mist in early summer or plant container-grown specimens in moist, strongly acidic to slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade in spring; Cornus kousa is significantly more resistant to dogwood anthracnose and borers than the native Cornus florida, making it the preferred choice for landscapes where these pests are a serious concern.

directions: Root softwood cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; Kousa dogwood is a Japanese, Korean, and Chinese small deciduous tree that blooms approximately three to four weeks later than the native flowering dogwood (Cornus florida) — producing its characteristic four-pointed, star-shaped, white or pink bracts in June rather than April — and is considered by many gardeners to be even more ornamental over the full year. The pointed, white (occasionally pink) bracts surround the small true flowers in the center and remain attractive for 4–6 weeks. In late summer and fall, large, bumpy, pink-red, strawberry-like composite fruit clusters ripen and are edible (sweet, slightly grainy, and pleasant-tasting, used to make jam in Japan). The fall foliage is outstanding — purplish-red to scarlet. On mature specimens the bark becomes attractively mottled and exfoliating. It is significantly more resistant to dogwood anthracnose disease and dogwood borer than C. florida — one of the primary reasons for preferring it in areas where these pests are problematic. 'Milky Way' (very heavy flower production), 'Satomi' (deep-rose-pink bracts), and 'Wolf Eyes' (white-variegated leaves) are outstanding cultivars.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in early summer under mist or plant container-grown specimens in moist, strongly acidic to slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade in spring; mountain silverbell grows naturally as an understory and sub-canopy tree in the southern Appalachian forests and is one of the finest shade-tolerant flowering trees available for eastern North American gardens.

directions: Root softwood cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; mountain silverbell is a large native deciduous tree of the southern Appalachian mountains that produces a spectacularly beautiful spring flower display in April and May, when the entire canopy becomes hung with thousands of small, nodding, pure-white, bell-shaped flowers that dangle in rows from the undersides of the leafing branches, backlit by new green leaves, creating one of the most delicate and ethereal spring effects of any native tree. The flowers are followed by decorative, four-winged, green to tan seed pods that persist through winter. In autumn the foliage turns clear yellow. Mountain silverbell grows naturally as a sub-canopy forest tree and is among the most shade-tolerant of all flowering trees — making it the ideal choice for planting under or alongside taller oaks, maples, and tulip poplars where most flowering trees would fail to bloom. The related Carolina silverbell (Halesia carolina) is similar but smaller; the mountain form (H. monticola, sometimes treated as H. tetraptera var. monticola) is the larger and more tree-like form. An outstanding native tree for the naturalistic woodland garden.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide or transplant root suckers in early spring, or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic, average to sandy, dry soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; sassafras transplants poorly due to its deep, fleshy taproot and is best established from small container-grown plants or root cuttings rather than field-dug specimens.

directions: Transplant root suckers or plant container specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic, dry to average soil in full sun to partial shade; sassafras is a native eastern North American deciduous tree distinguished from all other trees by its uniquely variable leaf shape — individual trees simultaneously bear three distinct leaf shapes: simple oval leaves, mitten-shaped leaves with one lobe, and three-lobed leaves — all on the same tree, all with the same distinctive, pleasantly spicy-aromatic, root-beer fragrance when crushed. In spring, small clusters of yellow flowers appear before the leaves. The autumn foliage is among the most brilliantly colored of any native tree — turning orange, red, purple, and scarlet in a spectacular multi-color display. Dark-blue, olive-like fruits on red stalks feed many songbirds in fall. Sassafras was historically important in American commerce — root bark was the original source of sassafras oil and the primary ingredient of traditional root beer and filé powder (an essential Creole seasoning) — though safrole, the active compound, is now restricted as a food additive. Spreads by root suckers to form groves. An excellent native tree for dry, sandy, difficult sites where many trees fail.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide root suckers in early spring or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average to humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade in spring; Allegheny serviceberry tolerates wetter and shadier conditions than most fruiting trees and is one of the best choices for a productive edible understory tree under tall oaks and maples.

directions: Divide root suckers or plant container specimens in moist, slightly acidic, average to humus-rich soil in partial shade; Allegheny serviceberry is a native eastern North American multi-stemmed small tree of exceptional four-season ornamental value — in early spring (March–April), before the leaves emerge, the smooth gray-barked stems become covered with clusters of delicate, drooping, pure-white, star-shaped flowers that are among the first tree flowers of the year; the bronze-red emerging leaves turn green in summer; round, sweet, dark-purple, blueberry-like fruits ripen in June (hence an alternative common name 'Juneberry') and are edible, sweet, and excellent fresh, in pies, jams, and for drying like raisins; and the foliage turns vivid orange-red to burgundy in autumn. As a native understory tree that grows naturally at forest edges and in open woodlands, Allegheny serviceberry is one of the most shade-tolerant fruiting trees available and will produce a useful fruit crop in partial shade conditions where no other small fruit tree would bear productively. The ripe berries are eagerly sought by at least 35 species of birds. Excellent combined with witch hazels and native azaleas in a naturalistic woodland edible garden.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile, well-drained soil in partial shade in spring; giant dogwood is best sited where its spectacular horizontal branching structure can be appreciated from a distance — avoid planting against structures where the distinctive horizontal form cannot be seen.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, slightly acidic, average to fertile soil in partial shade; giant dogwood is an East Asian deciduous tree considered by many plantspeople to be the finest and most architecturally distinguished large dogwood species — it develops a uniquely spectacular, perfectly horizontal, tiered, layered branching structure in which each layer of branches extends in a flat, table-like plane from the central trunk, creating a dramatically architectural silhouette reminiscent of a cedar of Lebanon in miniature, but made entirely of horizontal, flat-planed foliage tiers. In early summer, the upper surfaces of all the horizontal branches become covered with flat, corymb-like clusters of small, creamy-white flowers (without the large bracts of C. kousa or C. florida). The oval, alternate leaves turn deep-red to burgundy in autumn, and small, round, blue-black fruits provide food for birds. The legendary cultivar 'Variegata' (Wedding Cake Tree), with leaves broadly and irregularly margined in cream-white on the horizontally tiered branches, is one of the most spectacular variegated trees in cultivation and is widely regarded as a masterpiece of the ornamental tree world.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
